title: Interstitial Lung Disease
authors: Gary M. Hunninghake, Ivan O. Rosas

items.title: Recognizing Clinical Presentations and Associated Conditions in ILD
items.content: Most patients with interstitial lung disease (ILD) present with progressive exertional dyspnea or a persistent dry cough. However, some ILDs are identified through non-respiratory symptoms such as skin thickening in systemic sclerosis or ulnar deviation in rheumatoid arthritis. Additionally, incidental findings from imaging studies like chest x-rays and CT scans can reveal ILD.
keyPoints: Progressive exertional dyspnea or persistent dry cough are common presentations
Non-respiratory symptoms (e.g., skin thickening in systemic sclerosis) may indicate ILD
Incidental findings from imaging studies can identify ILDs
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Interstitial Lung Diseases
items.content: Diagnosing ILDs requires a multidisciplinary approach due to overlapping clinical, imaging, and histopathologic findings. No single piece of data is sufficient for diagnosis; instead, a combination of clinical data, laboratory studies, pulmonary function tests, imaging, and histopathology are necessary.
keyPoints: Multidisciplinary approach recommended for ILD diagnosis
No single diagnostic test confers a definitive diagnosis alone
Combination of clinical data, lab studies, pulmonary function testing, imaging, and histopathology required
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.title: Categorization and Prevalence of ILDs
items.content: ILDs can be categorized into known and unknown causes. Known causes include occupational exposures, medications, and systemic diseases. Unknown causes encompass rare disorders with classic presentations and idiopathic interstitial pneumonias (IIPs). Common forms of ILD include IPF, sarcoidosis, and those related to connective tissue diseases.
keyPoints: ILDs categorized into known and unknown causes
Known causes: occupational exposures, medications, systemic diseases
Common ILDs: IPF, sarcoidosis, ILDs related to connective tissue diseases
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.title: Age as a Predictor for IPF
items.content: The age of onset significantly influences the likelihood of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF). Patients over 60 are most commonly affected, while it is rare in those under 50.
keyPoints: IPF occurs most commonly in patients aged >60.
IPF is quite rare among patients aged <50.

In patients aged >65 without strong evidence for an alternate diagnosis, atypical chest CT findings are more likely to result in a histopathologic diagnosis of usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) than another idiopathic interstitial pneumonia (IIP).
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH | HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Sex Differences in ILD Prevalence
items.content: The likelihood of certain ILDs varies by sex, with some conditions more prevalent among women and others among men.
keyPoints: LAM is frequently diagnosed in young women.
Many CTD-associated ILDs are more common among women.
RA-associated ILD is more common among men.
IPF and occupational/exposure-related ILDs are more common among men.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH | HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Importance of Family History in ILD
items.content: A family history of interstitial lung disease is a significant risk factor for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.
keyPoints: The percentage of familial pulmonary fibrosis could be as high as 20%.
Having a close relative with an IIP is among the strongest risk factors for IPF.
Genetic studies show familial aggregation of diverse forms of IIP, including IPF, NSIP, and DIP.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH | HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Importance of Smoking History in ILD
items.content: A history of smoking is crucial in diagnosing certain forms of interstitial lung disease (ILD), such as respiratory bronchiolitis and desquamative interstitial pneumonia (DIP). It is also present in about three-quarters of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) patients. Occupational and environmental exposures should be assessed for potential links to ILD.
keyPoints: Smoking history is nearly always present in respiratory bronchiolitis and DIP.
Approximately 75% of IPF patients have a smoking history.
Occupational/environmental exposure histories are important for identifying causes like asbestos or hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY STUDIES | PULMONARY FUNCTION TESTS

items.title: Physical Examination Findings in ILD
items.content: End-inspiratory fine crackles at the lung bases are common early signs of IPF, though they are nonspecific. Wheezing is rare but may occur in conditions like sarcoidosis and hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Advanced disease may present with cyanosis, digital clubbing, and cor pulmonale.
keyPoints: End-inspiratory fine crackles at lung bases are common in IPF.
Rales are nonspecific and can be seen in various ILDs.
Wheezing is uncommon but may occur in sarcoidosis, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, and eosinophilic GPA.
Advanced disease signs include cyanosis, digital clubbing, and cor pulmonale.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY STUDIES | PULMONARY FUNCTION TESTS

items.title: Role of HRCT in ILD Diagnosis and Management
items.content: High-resolution CT (HRCT) is the standard initial imaging modality for suspected interstitial lung disease (ILD). It can be diagnostic for certain ILDs, such as idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), especially when combined with appropriate clinical context. HRCT helps define the extent of ILD, identify advanced disease features like honeycombing, and detect coexisting conditions such as emphysema or lung cancer. When non-diagnostic, it guides optimal biopsy locations.
keyPoints: HRCT is standard care for initial evaluation of suspected ILD.
Can be diagnostic for some ILDs (e.g., IPF) in the right context.
Helps define extent and advanced features like honeycombing.
Detects coexisting diseases such as emphysema or lung cancer.
Guides biopsy locations when non-diagnostic.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · CHEST IMAGING STUDIES | LUNG BIOPSY

items.title: Considerations for Surgical Lung Biopsy in ILD Diagnosis
items.content: Surgical lung biopsies, often performed via video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS), are crucial for diagnosing ILDs when non-invasive methods fail. They have a higher diagnostic yield if done before treatment initiation. However, the procedure carries risks, including up to 5% short-term mortality, which is higher in patients diagnosed with IPF or those presenting acutely.
keyPoints: Surgical lung biopsies are often performed via VATS.
Higher diagnostic yield when obtained prior to treatment.
Short-term mortality risk can be as high as 5%.
Risks are higher in patients diagnosed with IPF or those presenting acutely.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · CHEST IMAGING STUDIES | LUNG BIOPSY

items.subtopic: INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Prognosis of IPF
items.content: Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF) is the most common interstitial lung disease of unknown cause, with a prevalence increasing with age. Diagnosis involves combining clinical presentation, pulmonary function tests, imaging, immune serologies, and histopathology. The prognosis is poor, with an estimated 50% survival over 3-5 years.
keyPoints: IPF is the most common ILD of unknown cause.
Prevalence increases with age, estimated at 50–200:100,000.
Commonly diagnosed in the fifth or sixth decade of life, affects men more than women.
Associated with a history of smoking or other environmental exposures.
Poor prognosis with an estimated 50% 3- to 5-year survival.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS

items.subtopic: INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS
items.title: Imaging and Histopathology in IPF Diagnosis
items.content: High-resolution CT (HRCT) findings for IPF include subpleural reticulation with a posterior basal predominance, honeycombing, and traction bronchiectasis, known as a UIP pattern. Extensive ground-glass opacities or upper lung predominance suggest alternative diagnoses. Histopathology via VATS biopsy shows subpleural reticulation, honeycomb changes, and fibroblast foci, consistent with UIP.
keyPoints: HRCT findings: subpleural reticulation, posterior basal predominance, honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis (UIP pattern).
Suspicious for alternative diagnosis if extensive ground-glass opacities or upper lung predominance are present.
Histopathology: subpleural reticulation, honeycomb changes, fibroblast foci (UIP).
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS

items.title: Idiopathic NSIP: Prognosis and Treatment Approach
items.content: Idiopathic NSIP is characterized by a relatively good prognosis, especially in patients with a predominantly cellular pattern. The 5-year survival rate exceeds 80%, with better outcomes for those without fibrosing patterns.
keyPoints: Idiopathic NSIP has a >80% 5-year survival rate.
Cellular NSIP pattern indicates a more favorable prognosis compared to fibrotic pattern.
Treatment often includes oral steroids, cytotoxic agents, and possibly antifibrotic therapy for progressive cases.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · NONSPECIFIC INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA | SMOKING-RELATED ILD

items.title: HRCT Findings in NSIP vs. Smoking-Related ILDs
items.content: NSIP typically presents with diffuse subpleural, symmetric ground-glass, and reticular opacities on HRCT, while smoking-related ILDs like RB-ILD show central bronchial wall thickening and centrilobular nodules.
keyPoints: NSIP: Common HRCT findings include ground-glass and reticular opacities with subpleural sparing.
Smoking-related ILDs (e.g., RB-ILD): Central bronchial wall thickening, centrilobular nodules, and ground-glass opacities are prominent.
Honeycombing is rare in smoking-related ILDs but indicates a worse prognosis.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · NONSPECIFIC INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA | SMOKING-RELATED ILD

items.title: Management of Smoking-Related ILDs
items.content: Smoking cessation is crucial for managing smoking-related ILDs. While immunosuppressive and cytotoxic agents have been studied, there's no strong evidence supporting their efficacy in improving symptoms or preventing deterioration.
keyPoints: Counseling for smoking cessation is essential for patients with smoking-related ILD.
Immunosuppressive (e.g., prednisone) and cytotoxic agents have limited evidence of efficacy.
No significant improvements in symptoms or pulmonary function measures from these therapies.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · NONSPECIFIC INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA | SMOKING-RELATED ILD

items.subtopic: CRYPTOGENIC ORGANIZING PNEUMONIA | ACUTE INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA (HAMMAN-RICH SYNDROME)
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia
items.content: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP) often presents in patients aged 50–60 with symptoms mimicking a subacute flu, including cough, dyspnea, fever, and fatigue. Inspiratory rales and restrictive lung deficits are typical findings. HRCT imaging aids diagnosis through identifying patchy consolidative opacities and ground-glass opacities, sometimes showing the reversed halo sign. Treatment primarily involves corticosteroids for at least 6 months due to high relapse rates, with potential use of alternate therapies like mycophenolate or rituximab.
keyPoints: COP typically affects patients aged 50–60
Symptoms include cough, dyspnea, fever, fatigue, and inspiratory rales
Pulmonary function tests show restrictive lung deficits with hypoxemia
HRCT findings: patchy consolidative opacities, ground-glass opacities, reversed halo sign
Histopathology shows organizing pneumonia with granulation tissue
Corticosteroids are primary treatment; alternate therapies may reduce steroid need
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · CRYPTOGENIC ORGANIZING PNEUMONIA | ACUTE INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA (HAMMAN-RICH SYNDROME)

items.subtopic: CRYPTOGENIC ORGANIZING PNEUMONIA | ACUTE INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA (HAMMAN-RICH SYNDROME)
items.title: Characteristics and Management of Acute Interstitial Pneumonia
items.content: Acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP), also known as Hamman-Rich syndrome, is a rare and often fatal condition with an acute onset of respiratory distress and hypoxemia. It can follow symptoms resembling an upper respiratory infection. The mortality rate exceeds 50% within six months, with frequent recurrences. AIP's HRCT findings include patchy bilateral ground-glass opacities and air-space consolidation. Histopathology reveals diffuse alveolar damage similar to ARDS. Management is primarily supportive, often involving mechanical ventilation, with glucocorticoids used controversially.
keyPoints: AIP presents acutely with respiratory distress and hypoxemia
Prodromal symptoms may mimic an upper respiratory infection
Mortality rate >50% within 6 months; recurrences are common
HRCT findings: patchy bilateral ground-glass opacities, air-space consolidation
Histopathology shows diffuse alveolar damage (DAD)
Treatment is supportive with mechanical ventilation; glucocorticoids' efficacy is controversial
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · CRYPTOGENIC ORGANIZING PNEUMONIA | ACUTE INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA (HAMMAN-RICH SYNDROME)

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Acute Exacerbations in ILD with Pulmonary Fibrosis
items.content: Acute exacerbations represent an accelerated phase of lung injury, most severe in patients with IPF. They are characterized by acute respiratory distress and hypoxemia within 30 days, not explained by other causes. Mortality is very high (>85%), with survival ranging from days to months.
keyPoints: Acute exacerbations occur in any ILD with pulmonary fibrosis but are most severe in IPF.
Characterized by acute onset (<30 days) of respiratory distress and hypoxemia.
Mortality rates are very high (>85%).
Mean survival periods range from days to months.
HRCT findings include patchy bilateral ground-glass opacities and air-space consolidation.
Histopathology shows diffuse alveolar damage (DAD) or organizing pneumonia.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · ACUTE EXACERBATIONS OF IIPS | ILD ASSOCIATED WITH CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE

items.subtopic: ILD ASSOCIATED WITH CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE
items.title: Understanding ILD in the Context of Connective Tissue Diseases
items.content: ILD is a common manifestation of CTDs, with variable progression and survival. It occurs most frequently in scleroderma (SSc), rheumatoid arthritis (RA), polymyositis/dermatomyositis, and less commonly in Sjögren's syndrome and SLE. ILD may precede or coincide with extrapulmonary manifestations of CTDs.
keyPoints: ILD is common in many CTDs, including scleroderma (SSc), RA, polymyositis/dermatomyositis.
Occurs less frequently in Sjögren's syndrome and SLE.
Disease progression, response to therapy, and survival vary with specific radiologic and histopathologic patterns.
ILD may precede or present alongside extrapulmonary manifestations of CTDs.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · ACUTE EXACERBATIONS OF IIPS | ILD ASSOCIATED WITH CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE

items.title: Interstitial Lung Disease in Systemic Sclerosis: Prevalence and Management
items.content: ILD is the most common pulmonary manifestation of systemic sclerosis (SSc), occurring in approximately 50% of patients with diffuse disease and 30% with limited disease. Pulmonary hypertension can also occur, more frequently in those with limited SSc. Treatment options include cyclophosphamide for modest lung function preservation, mycophenolate for similar efficacy with better tolerability, and emerging therapies like tocilizumab and nintedanib. Managing gastroesophageal reflux is crucial to minimize lung injury.
keyPoints: ILD occurs in ~50% of SSc patients with diffuse disease and ~30% with limited disease.
Pulmonary hypertension is more frequent in patients with limited SSc.
Cyclophosphamide has modest benefit but significant toxicity; mycophenolate offers similar efficacy with improved tolerability.
Anti-interleukin 6 (e.g., tocilizumab) and antifibrotic therapy (e.g., nintedanib) are emerging treatments.
High-dose proton pump inhibitors or antireflux surgery should be considered to minimize reflux-related lung injury.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS | RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS

items.title: Interstitial Lung Disease in Rheumatoid Arthritis: Prevalence, Patterns, and Treatment
items.content: ILD is a common extraarticular complication of RA, occurring clinically in nearly 10% but radiologically in up to 40-50%, suggesting underdiagnosis. It's more frequent in males and those with tobacco exposure. The UIP pattern on HRCT is most common, associated with decreased survival, while NSIP patterns are also observed. There are no specific trials for RA-ILD treatment; therapies include immunosuppressive agents like prednisone and mycophenolate, with variable success. Lung transplantation may be considered for progressive disease.
keyPoints: Clinically evident RA-ILD occurs in nearly 10% of the RA population; radiologic abnormalities suggest up to 40–50% prevalence.
RA-ILD is more frequent in males and those with a history of tobacco exposure.
The most common HRCT pattern for RA-ILD is UIP, associated with reduced survival.
No specific clinical trials for RA-ILD treatment; therapies include immunosuppressive and cytotoxic agents.
Lung transplantation is an option for eligible patients with progressive disease unresponsive to medical therapy.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS | RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS

items.title: ILD Prevalence in Anti-Synthetase Syndrome and Associated Antibodies
items.content: Interstitial lung disease (ILD) is prevalent in up to 45% of patients with dermatomyositis/polymyositis who have positive anti-synthetase antibodies. The presence of specific antibodies, such as anti-Jo-1 or anti-PL-12, can influence the pattern and progression of ILD.
keyPoints: ILD is present in up to 45% of patients with positive anti-synthetase antibodies.
Anti-Jo-1 antibody-positive individuals may develop rapidly progressive ILD.
ILD may be more common among those with anti-PL-12 antibodies.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DERMATOMYOSITIS/POLYMYOSITIS | GRANULOMATOUS ILDS

items.title: Imaging and Histopathology Patterns in Dermatomyositis/Polymyositis-Associated ILD
items.content: HRCT imaging of ILD in dermatomyositis/polymyositis typically shows patterns consistent with NSIP, COP, or UIP. The anti-synthetase syndrome can present with multiple histopathologic subtypes including NSIP, COP, and UIP, with DAD associated with rapidly progressive ILD.
keyPoints: Common HRCT imaging patterns include NSIP, COP, and UIP.
Anti-synthetase syndrome is linked to NSIP, COP, and UIP histopathology.
DAD is associated with rapidly progressive ILD in myositis patients.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DERMATOMYOSITIS/POLYMYOSITIS | GRANULOMATOUS ILDS

items.title: Genetic Predisposition to IPF: The Role of MUC5B Variant
items.content: The presence of a mucin 5B (MUC5B) promoter variant significantly increases the risk for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF), highlighting its role in genetic predisposition. This variant is common in Caucasian populations and even more prevalent among IPF patients, suggesting it as a key target for early detection strategies.
keyPoints: At least one copy of MUC5B promoter variant present in ~20% of Caucasian populations.
35–45% prevalence of the MUC5B variant in patients with IPF.
Conveys an approximate sixfold increase in risk for IPF.
Imaging abnormalities suggestive of early pulmonary fibrosis are found in ~7–9% of adults without known ILD, associated with MUC5B.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · GENETICS AND ILD | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Racial Differences and Genetic Variants in ILD
items.content: There are notable racial differences in the genetic attributes of interstitial lung diseases (ILD), particularly between Caucasian and Asian populations. This includes variations in the prevalence of specific genetic variants like MUC5B, which affects clinical outcomes such as the rate of acute exacerbations.
keyPoints: Racial differences exist in ILD clinical presentation and genetics between Caucasian and Asian populations.
Limited data on ILD prevalence among Hispanics, subjects of African descent, and other ethnic groups.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · GENETICS AND ILD | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which symptom is commonly reported by patients ultimately diagnosed with an interstitial lung disease (ILD)?
options: Progressive exertional dyspnea or a persistent dry cough
items.explanation: Most patients with ILD report progressive exertional dyspnea or a persistent dry cough.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.question: What is the central tenet of diagnosing interstitial lung diseases (ILDs)?
options: A single piece of data confers a diagnosis alone.
Clinical data, laboratory studies, pulmonary function testing, imaging findings, and histopathology are jointly required to make a confident diagnosis.
Only histopathological examination is necessary for diagnosis.
Diagnosis can be made based solely on clinical presentation.
items.explanation: A confident ILD diagnosis requires the combined weight of multiple data sources, not just one.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.question: Which approach is recommended by most ILD centers for diagnosing and managing interstitial lung diseases?
options: Unilateral decision-making by a single specialist.
A multidisciplinary approach involving pulmonologists, rheumatologists, radiologists, and pathologists.
Diagnosis based solely on imaging findings.
items.explanation: Most ILD centers recommend a multidisciplinary approach for diagnosis and management.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.question: Which of the following is an example of a known cause of interstitial lung disease?
options: Occupational exposure to asbestos leading to asbestosis
items.explanation: Asbestosis is a known cause of ILD due to occupational exposure.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.question: Which age group is most commonly affected by IPF?
items.explanation: IPF occurs most commonly in patients aged >60.

items.question: Which ILD is more common among young women?
items.explanation: LAM is frequently diagnosed in young women.

items.question: What symptom might suggest an ILD associated with diffuse alveolar hemorrhage?
items.explanation: Cough with hemoptysis could suggest an ILD associated with diffuse alveolar hemorrhage.

items.question: Which medication is known to cause ILD and is used in treating inflammatory lung disease?
items.explanation: Methotrexate, among others, is used to treat inflammatory lung disease and can cause ILD.

items.question: Which form of interstitial lung disease is most commonly associated with a history of smoking?
items.explanation: A history of smoking is nearly always present in respiratory bronchiolitis and DIP, which are referred to as smoking-related ILD.

items.question: Which physical examination finding may be one of the earliest signs of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF)?
items.explanation: End-inspiratory fine crackles, or rales, noted at the lung bases are often one of the earliest signs of IPF.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY STUDIES | PULMONARY FUNCTION TESTS

items.question: Which pulmonary function test finding is unusual in many forms of interstitial lung disease but can be present with an additional restrictive deficit?
options: Reduction in the diffusing capacity for carbon monoxide (DLCO)
items.explanation: A reduced FEV1 to FVC ratio is unusual in many forms of ILD but can be present with an additional restrictive deficit in some ILDs involving the airways.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY STUDIES | PULMONARY FUNCTION TESTS

items.question: Which imaging study is considered the standard of care for initial evaluation of a patient suspected to have interstitial lung disease (ILD)?
items.explanation: HRCT is considered the standard of care for initial evaluation in suspected ILD.

items.question: What can HRCT chest imaging help determine in patients with interstitial lung disease?
options: The specific genetic mutation causing the ILD
The presence of more concerning features suggestive of advanced disease
The exact dosage of medication required for treatment
The patient's response to previous treatments
items.explanation: HRCT can help determine the presence of advanced disease features like honeycombing.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · CHEST IMAGING STUDIES | LUNG BIOPSY

items.question: What is a limitation of bronchoscopically obtained tissue samples in diagnosing interstitial lung diseases?
options: They are always diagnostic for all ILDs.
They often provide insufficient material to diagnose most idiopathic interstitial pneumonias (IIPs).
They can only be used after treatment has started.
items.explanation: Bronchoscopically obtained tissue samples are often insufficient to diagnose most IIPs.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · CHEST IMAGING STUDIES | LUNG BIOPSY

items.subtopic: INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS
items.question: Which imaging findings are collectively referred to as a UIP pattern in IPF?
options: Extensive ground-glass opacities and bronchovascular changes
Honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, and subpleural reticulation with posterior basal predominance
items.explanation: The UIP pattern includes honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis, and subpleural reticulation with a posterior basal predominance.

items.question: Which of the following treatments has been shown to be associated with increased morbidity and mortality in IPF patients?
items.explanation: Treatment with immunosuppression has been shown to be associated with increased morbidity and mortality in IPF patients.

items.explanation: Peribronchiolar thickening is uncommon in idiopathic NSIP.

items.explanation: Idiopathic NSIP is often treated with oral steroids and cytotoxic agents.

options: Variable, depending on age and gender
items.explanation: Idiopathic NSIP has a relatively good prognosis with a 5-year survival of >80%.

options: Subacute flulike illness with cough, dyspnea, fever, and fatigue
Acute onset of respiratory distress without preceding symptoms

items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS | RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS

items.question: Which therapy has been recently shown to have similar efficacy and improved tolerability compared to cyclophosphamide for systemic sclerosis-associated ILD?
items.explanation: Mycophenolate has shown similar efficacy and improved tolerability compared to cyclophosphamide in SSc-associated ILD.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS | RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS

items.question: What is the most common imaging pattern of interstitial lung disease (ILD) in patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA)?
items.explanation: The UIP pattern is the most common imaging pattern of ILD in RA patients.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS | RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS

items.question: Which antibody is associated with a rapidly progressive form of interstitial lung disease in patients with dermatomyositis/polymyositis?
items.explanation: A subset of anti-Jo-1 antibody-positive individuals develop a rapidly progressive form of ILD.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DERMATOMYOSITIS/POLYMYOSITIS | GRANULOMATOUS ILDS

items.question: What is the most common histopathologic pattern associated with anti-synthetase syndrome in dermatomyositis/polymyositis patients?
items.explanation: The anti-synthetase syndrome is associated with multiple histopathologic subtypes including NSIP.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DERMATOMYOSITIS/POLYMYOSITIS | GRANULOMATOUS ILDS

items.question: Which imaging pattern of interstitial lung disease may be more common among patients with anti-PL-12 antibodies?
items.explanation: A UIP pattern may be more common among those with anti-PL-12 antibodies.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DERMATOMYOSITIS/POLYMYOSITIS | GRANULOMATOUS ILDS

items.question: Which genetic variant is most strongly associated with both familial pulmonary fibrosis (FPF) and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF)?
items.explanation: The MUC5B promoter variant is strongly associated with both FPF and IPF.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · GENETICS AND ILD | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: What percentage of Caucasian populations approximately carry at least one copy of the MUC5B promoter variant?
items.explanation: About 20% of Caucasian populations have at least one copy of the MUC5B promoter variant.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · GENETICS AND ILD | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which gene is NOT mentioned as being associated with genetic variants in pulmonary fibrosis?
items.explanation: The IL12B gene is not mentioned as being associated with genetic variants in pulmonary fibrosis.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · GENETICS AND ILD | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: Interstitial lung diseases can be diagnosed based solely on histopathology.
items.explanation: Diagnosis requires a combination of clinical data, laboratory studies, pulmonary function testing, imaging findings, and histopathology.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.statement: Granulomatous lung diseases can be caused by both known and unknown factors.
items.explanation: Granulomatous lung diseases include hypersensitivity pneumonitis (known cause) and sarcoidosis (unknown cause).
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.statement: IPF is quite rare among patients aged <50.
items.explanation: IPF occurs most commonly in patients aged >60 and is quite rare among those aged <50.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH | HISTORY (part 1)

items.statement: Cough with hemoptysis is common in IPF.
items.explanation: Cough with hemoptysis is rare and could suggest other conditions like DAH or LAM.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH | HISTORY (part 1)

items.statement: Wheezing is a common finding in most forms of interstitial lung disease.
items.explanation: Wheezing is uncommon in most forms of ILD but can be present in some disorders like sarcoidosis and hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY STUDIES | PULMONARY FUNCTION TESTS

items.statement: A reduction in the diffusing capacity for carbon monoxide (DLCO) is specific for interstitial lung disease.
items.explanation: There is more measurement variability in DLCO, and it is less specific for ILD.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · HISTORY (part 2) | PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY STUDIES | PULMONARY FUNCTION TESTS

items.statement: Chest X-rays alone rarely lead to a specific diagnosis of interstitial lung disease.
items.explanation: CXR findings are often not sufficient for a specific ILD diagnosis.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · CHEST IMAGING STUDIES | LUNG BIOPSY

items.statement: Surgical lung biopsies always carry no risk of mortality.
items.explanation: There is a short-term mortality rate associated with surgical lung biopsies, up to 5%.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · CHEST IMAGING STUDIES | LUNG BIOPSY

items.subtopic: INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS
items.statement: IPF is most commonly diagnosed in the third or fourth decade of life.
items.explanation: IPF is commonly diagnosed in the fifth or sixth decade of life.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS

items.subtopic: INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS
items.statement: Antifibrotic therapy can slow the decline of lung function in IPF patients.
items.explanation: Large clinical trials have demonstrated that antifibrotic therapy (pirfenidone and nintedanib) can slow the decline of lung function in IPF patients.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS

items.statement: Honeycombing is a common finding in RB-ILD.
items.explanation: Honeycombing is generally felt to be rare in RB-ILD.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · NONSPECIFIC INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA | SMOKING-RELATED ILD

items.statement: Idiopathic NSIP predominantly occurs in nonsmoking females.
items.explanation: Idiopathic NSIP is commonly diagnosed in nonsmoking females.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · NONSPECIFIC INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA | SMOKING-RELATED ILD

items.subtopic: CRYPTOGENIC ORGANIZING PNEUMONIA | ACUTE INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA (HAMMAN-RICH SYNDROME)
items.statement: Acute Interstitial Pneumonia (AIP) has a high mortality rate within 6 months of presentation.
items.explanation: The mortality rate for AIP can be quite high (>50%) within 6 months.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · CRYPTOGENIC ORGANIZING PNEUMONIA | ACUTE INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA (HAMMAN-RICH SYNDROME)

items.subtopic: CRYPTOGENIC ORGANIZING PNEUMONIA | ACUTE INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA (HAMMAN-RICH SYNDROME)
items.statement: Glucocorticoids are clearly effective in treating Acute Interstitial Pneumonia (AIP).
items.explanation: There is no proven drug therapy for AIP, and glucocorticoids are not clearly effective.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · CRYPTOGENIC ORGANIZING PNEUMONIA | ACUTE INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA (HAMMAN-RICH SYNDROME)

items.subtopic: ACUTE EXACERBATIONS OF IIPS | ILD ASSOCIATED WITH CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE
items.statement: Acute exacerbations of IPF are characterized by an acute onset of respiratory distress and hypoxemia occurring in a patient with underlying pulmonary fibrosis not explained by an alternate cause.
items.explanation: This statement accurately describes the clinical manifestations of acute exacerbations of IPF.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · ACUTE EXACERBATIONS OF IIPS | ILD ASSOCIATED WITH CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE

items.subtopic: ACUTE EXACERBATIONS OF IIPS | ILD ASSOCIATED WITH CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE
items.statement: Mechanical ventilation is universally recommended for all patients experiencing an acute exacerbation of pulmonary fibrosis.
items.explanation: The use of mechanical ventilation in the context of acute exacerbations, when not used as a bridge to lung transplantation, is controversial due to poor survival rates.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · ACUTE EXACERBATIONS OF IIPS | ILD ASSOCIATED WITH CONNECTIVE TISSUE DISEASE

items.statement: Interstitial lung disease occurs in nearly 10% of the rheumatoid arthritis population with clinically evident RA-ILD.
items.explanation: Clinically evident RA-ILD occurs in nearly 10% of the RA population.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS | RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS

items.statement: RA patients with progressive pulmonary fibrosis typically experience a significant decline in lung function in response to antifibrotic therapy.
items.explanation: RA patients with progressive pulmonary fibrosis may have less of a decline in lung function in response to antifibrotic therapy.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS | RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS

items.statement: Sarcoidosis is the most common granulomatous interstitial lung disease.
items.explanation: The passage states that sarcoidosis is the most common granulomatous ILD.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DERMATOMYOSITIS/POLYMYOSITIS | GRANULOMATOUS ILDS

items.statement: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) is caused by inhalation of inorganic dusts only.
items.explanation: HP can be due to both organic and inorganic dusts.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DERMATOMYOSITIS/POLYMYOSITIS | GRANULOMATOUS ILDS

items.statement: Genetic studies have shown that imaging abnormalities suggestive of early-stage pulmonary fibrosis are uncommon in adults without known interstitial lung disease.
items.explanation: Imaging abnormalities suggestive of an early stage of pulmonary fibrosis occur in ~7–9% of adults.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · GENETICS AND ILD | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: Genome-wide association studies have identified genetic variants associated with sarcoidosis that are involved in T-cell activation.
items.explanation: Some disease-associated variants in sarcoidosis fall in regions of genes involved in T-cell activation.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · GENETICS AND ILD | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis is the most common form of interstitial lung disease.
items.reason: IPF, along with sarcoidosis and ILDs related to connective tissue diseases, are among the most common forms of ILD.
items.explanation: Both IPF and other conditions like sarcoidosis and CTD-related ILDs are common, but IPF is not singularly the most common.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.assertion: A lung biopsy demonstrating usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern confirms a diagnosis of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.
items.reason: The UIP pattern can also be present in some connective tissue diseases, not just IPF.
items.explanation: While helpful, the UIP pattern alone does not confirm IPF as it can occur in other conditions.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · Interstitial Lung Disease

items.assertion: A family history of ILD is among the strongest risk factors for IPF.
items.reason: Studies suggest a close relative with an IIP increases the risk for IPF.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH | HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: Respiratory symptoms like progressive dyspnea are uncommon in ILDs.
items.reason: Progressive dyspnea is the most common complaint in patients with ILD.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because respiratory symptoms like progressive dyspnea are common, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · DIAGNOSTIC APPROACH | HISTORY (part 1)

items.subtopic: INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS
items.assertion: IPF is associated with a history of smoking or other environmental exposures.
items.reason: IPF affects men more than women and is frequently linked to environmental factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why IPF is associated with these risk factors.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · INDIVIDUAL FORMS OF ILD | IDIOPATHIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS

items.assertion: Patients with a predominant cellular NSIP pattern have a more favorable prognosis.
items.reason: Cellular patterns are potentially more responsive to medical therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · NONSPECIFIC INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA | SMOKING-RELATED ILD

items.assertion: Prolonged survival is expected in most patients with RB-ILD.
items.reason: Death secondary to progressive ILD is common in RB-ILD.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; death due to progressive ILD is rare in RB-ILD.
items.reference: Interstitial Lung Disease · NONSPECIFIC INTERSTITIAL PNEUMONIA | SMOKING-RELATED ILD
